unicode.value: Unit 4: Problem solving at work - Session 3

Todas las imágenes incluidas en esta unidad han sido generadas mediante inteligencia artificial a través de la herramienta ChatGPT (OpenAI). Estas imágenes han sido creadas exclusivamente para uso educativo y no reproducen contenido preexistente. No están sujetas a derechos de autor de terceros y pueden ser utilizadas dentro del marco del proyecto educativo sin fines comerciales. Se aplican los términos de uso y licencias correspondientes al servicio de generación de imágenes de OpenAI, que permiten su uso para fines educativos y de desarrollo de materiales propios.
Unit 4: Problem solving at work - Session 3

🧭 Session 3 – Reflecting, Explaining, and Responding

Why This Matters

💼 When workplace problems arise, solving them is only part of the job—reflecting on what happened and communicating clearly with others is just as important. This session equips you with the skills to describe issues, analyze causes, and propose solutions professionally, whether in writing or speaking.

💡 Tip: Clear communication builds trust and shows professionalism.

✅ What You’ll Do

You’ll develop grammar, writing, and speaking skills to handle workplace challenges effectively. In this session, you will:

  • Review third and mixed conditionals to discuss past events and their present impacts.
  • Practice written communication to request support from a supplier or respond to a client.
  • Build oral communication skills to reflect on incidents and give advice with clear structure and tone.

These activities, aligned with CEFR communication strategies, prepare you for professional interactions like emails or team discussions.

💡 Tip: Focus on clarity and structure to communicate confidently.

🚀 Getting Started

This session includes lessons on grammar, writing, and speaking, plus practice tasks to apply your skills, such as writing to a supplier and advising a coworker. Start with the grammar lesson to review conditionals, then explore the writing and speaking sections to build your communication skills.

💡 Tip: Use Unit 4 vocabulary like “sort out” or “escalate” to sound professional.

Use of English – Third and Mixed Conditionals

Reflecting on Past Situations and Their Consequences

At work, things don’t always go as planned. A missed deadline, a technical error, a forgotten task… In these moments, we often think about what could have happened if the situation had been different. That’s when third and mixed conditionals are useful.

In this section, you’ll explore how to talk about:

  • Imagined alternatives to past actions (third conditional)
  • How a past action affects the present (mixed conditional)
  • Real workplace situations where this grammar is essential

📚 What You’ll Do

You’ll work through three parts:

  • 1️⃣ Diagnostic Test
    Find out what you already know — and where you need more practice — by identifying and correcting typical conditional structures.
  • 2️⃣ Grammar Focus
    Explore third and mixed conditionals from a cognitive perspective: how we mentally connect events across time, and how language reflects imagined consequences and responsibility.
  • 3️⃣ Guided Practice
    Apply your knowledge through real work-based examples. You’ll notice how the grammar is used in professional communication and learn to use it yourself with more confidence.

🧠 This section will help you develop accuracy, clarity, and flexibility when describing problems, making polite suggestions, or reflecting on decisions — key skills in any workplace.

Diagnostic Test – Third and Mixed Conditionals

🧠 Get Ready to Check Your Grammar!

Before you move on to real workplace tasks, take a moment to check how confident you are using conditional sentences that describe past situations and their consequences.

This diagnostic test focuses on:

  • ✅ Third conditional – to imagine how things could have been different in the past
  • ✅ Mixed conditionals – to connect a past situation to a present result

This isn’t a test for marks — it’s a tool to help you understand your progress and learning needs.

🎯 What’s the Goal?

To help you decide if you can use third and mixed conditionals confidently — or if you need a quick review before using them in writing, emails or spoken reflections at work.

📘 How Does It Work?

  • ✍️ Complete 20 sentences by choosing or writing the correct form.
  • 🔎 Check your answers.
  • 🧠 Reflect on how meaning changes with each structure.

🧮 Scoring

  • 80% or more → Great! You’re ready for real tasks and confident language use.
  • Less than 80% → No worries! Review the grammar section and practise with the activities — you’ll feel more secure in no time.

🌟 What You’ll Gain

This activity helps you develop:

  • 🎓 Competence to Learn to Learn – Identify what you’ve mastered and what needs review
  • 🌱 Lifelong Learning Competence – Gain awareness of how to keep growing as a user of English

🚀 Let’s begin and see how much you already know!

Diagnostic Test – Third and Mixed Conditionals

Instructions: Complete the test by filling in the blanks, choosing the correct option, or correcting errors. There are 20 items. Click "Check Answers" for each part to see feedback. At the end, check your score. If you score 80% or higher (16/20), you can skip this grammar point.

Part A – Complete the Sentence

Instructions: Type the correct verb forms to complete each third or mixed conditional sentence. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. If we ______ (schedule) the delivery, the client ______ (not/wait) now.
  • 2. If I ______ (attend) the meeting, I ______ (understand) the plan yesterday.
  • 3. If they ______ (test) the product, it ______ (not/fail) last week.
  • 4. If she ______ (review) the data, we ______ (not/be) confused now.
  • 5. If we ______ (hire) more staff, we ______ (complete) the project on time.
  • 6. If I ______ (back up) the files, we ______ (not/lose) the data now.

💡 Tip: Use “had + past participle” for the ‘if’ clause; use “would have + past participle” for past results or “would + base verb” for present results.

Part B – Choose the Correct Option

Instructions: Select the correct option to complete each sentence. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 7. If we had followed the plan, we _____ the deadline.
  • 8. If I had studied the manual, I _____ how to fix this now.
  • 9. If they had shipped the order, the client _____ now.
  • 10. If we had prepared the report, we _____ it yesterday.
  • 11. If I had confirmed the booking, we _____ a room now.
  • 12. If he had listened to advice, he _____ the mistake last week.
  • 13. If she had contacted the supplier, we _____ short of materials now.

💡 Tip: Third conditionals use “would have + past participle” for past results; mixed conditionals use “would + base verb” for present results.

Part C – Find and Correct the Mistake

Instructions: Each sentence has one grammar error. Type the corrected sentence in the text box. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 14. ❌ If I would have prepared better, I wouldn’t be nervous now.
  • 15. ❌ If we had informed the team, we won’t be behind schedule.
  • 16. ❌ If she checked the document, it would have been perfect yesterday.
  • 17. ❌ If you had been more careful, you wouldn’t made the error.
  • 18. ❌ If they would have tested the system, it wouldn’t crash now.
  • 19. ❌ If I had saved the file, I won’t be redoing it now.
  • 20. ❌ If he had arrived on time, we would finished the meeting early.

💡 Tip: Look for errors in verb forms or tense consistency in the ‘if’ or result clauses.

Your Score

Click below to calculate your total score across all parts.

💡 Tip: A score of 80% or higher (16/20 correct) means you can skip this grammar point.

Grammar Focus – Third and Mixed Conditionals

💡 Why These Structures Matter

In the workplace, we often reflect on past mistakes to learn what could have been done differently or how they’re affecting us now. Third and mixed conditionals help you talk about these “what if” scenarios in a professional way, whether you’re reviewing a project, apologizing to a client, or discussing improvements with your team.

This session explains these grammar structures by focusing on their meaning in real work situations. You’ll see how they help you express ideas about the past and present clearly and professionally.


🎧 Complementary Audio – Guided Explanation
This audio supports you as you go through the lesson. Listen to it for a step-by-step explanation with spoken examples. It’s especially helpful if you prefer learning by listening or want to review while doing other tasks.

🧠 Third Conditional: Imagining a Different Past

The third conditional lets you talk about things that didn’t happen in the past and imagine what could have been different. It’s useful for reflecting on mistakes or missed opportunities at work.

Structure: If + had + past participle, would have + past participle

Example: If we had checked the order carefully, we wouldn’t have sent the wrong items.

(Reality: We didn’t check carefully, so we sent the wrong items.)

What This Means

When you use the third conditional, you’re imagining a different version of the past, like rewinding a movie and changing the ending. The “if” part describes what didn’t happen, and the “would have” part shows the result you’re imagining. This helps you explain what went wrong or take responsibility in a professional way.

💡 Tip: Use the third conditional in team meetings or reports to reflect on past actions, like explaining why a project failed.

🔁 Mixed Conditionals: Connecting Past and Present

Mixed conditionals show how something that happened (or didn’t happen) in the past affects things now. They’re great for explaining why you’re facing a problem today because of a past mistake.

Structure: If + had + past participle, would + base verb

Example: If we had fixed the system last week, we wouldn’t be losing time now.

(Reality: We didn’t fix the system, so we’re losing time today.)

What This Means

Mixed conditionals link two different times in your mind: something that went wrong in the past and how it’s causing trouble now. The “if” part looks back at the past mistake, and the “would” part describes the current situation. This helps you explain to colleagues or clients why things are the way they are.

💡 Tip: Use mixed conditionals when apologizing to clients or discussing ongoing issues caused by past decisions.

🛠 Workplace Examples

Third and mixed conditionals are common when analyzing mistakes or explaining problems at work. Here’s how they’re used in real-life situations:

Situation Third Conditional Mixed Conditional
A delivery was late If we had sent the package on time, it would have arrived yesterday. If we had sent the package earlier, the client wouldn’t be upset now.
An email was missed If I had sent the email, the team would have been prepared. If I had sent the email, we wouldn’t be confused today.
A deadline was missed If we had started earlier, we would have finished the report. If we had planned better, we wouldn’t be behind now.

💡 Tip: Use these structures in emails or discussions to sound thoughtful and professional when talking about past mistakes.

🧠 Exercise 1 – Identify the Mental Timeline

Instructions: Read each sentence and select whether it describes a past cause + past consequence (third conditional) or past cause + present consequence (mixed conditional). Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. If we had updated the software, the system wouldn’t be crashing now.
  • 2. If I had replied to the client, we would have secured the deal yesterday.
  • 3. If they had trained the staff, we wouldn’t be short-staffed today.
  • 4. If she had submitted the proposal, it would have been approved last week.

💡 Tip: Third conditionals imagine a different past; mixed conditionals link a past mistake to a present problem.

🧠 Exercise 2 – Match the Scenario to the Mental Simulation

Instructions: Match each workplace scenario to the correct conditional sentence by selecting from the dropdown. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

Scenario Sentence Feedback
You missed a deadline, causing stress now.
A wrong order was sent due to a past error.
The client was satisfied because you acted promptly in the past.
A project failed due to poor planning.

💡 Tip: Match the timeline in the scenario to the sentence: past consequences for third conditionals, present consequences for mixed.

🧠 Exercise 3 – Complete the Sentence

Instructions: Complete each sentence by typing the correct third or mixed conditional form in the text box. Use the verbs provided. Click "Check Answers" for feedback.

  • 1. If we ______ (inform) the team earlier, we ______ (avoid) the confusion now.
  • 2. If I ______ (review) the contract, it ______ (be) signed yesterday.
  • 3. If they ______ (schedule) the meeting properly, we ______ (not/rush) today.
  • 4. If she ______ (test) the equipment, it ______ (work) during the presentation.

💡 Tip: Use “had + past participle” for the ‘if’ clause; use “would have + past participle” for past results or “would + base verb” for present results.

Extra Practice – Web Resources for Third and Mixed Conditionals

🎯 Why Use These Resources?

To master third and mixed conditionals, practice is key. These online resources offer interactive exercises, clear explanations, and practical examples to help you use these structures confidently in workplace scenarios, such as project reviews, client apologies, or team discussions. Each website below provides activities that reinforce the grammar from Session 3, focusing on professional communication.

💡 Tip: As you use these resources, try to apply the conditionals to real workplace situations, like explaining a missed deadline or reflecting on a project mistake.

📚 Recommended Web Resources

Explore these trusted websites for additional practice with third and mixed conditionals. They offer a variety of exercises, from gap-fills to quizzes, designed for intermediate learners like you.

1. British Council – LearnEnglish

The British Council’s LearnEnglish site offers clear explanations of third and mixed conditionals, with interactive exercises like multiple-choice questions and gap-fills. The examples are practical and relevant, helping you understand how to use these structures in everyday communication.

Link: Conditionals: Third and Mixed

Why It’s Useful: The site includes workplace-relevant examples, such as “If we had moved faster, we would have met the deadline,” and interactive tasks to test your understanding.

💡 Tip: Try the grammar test after reading the explanation to check your progress.

2. Test-English

Test-English provides detailed grammar explanations and a variety of exercises, including gap-fills and multiple-choice quizzes, for mixed conditionals. The site focuses on both past-to-present and present-to-past hypothetical scenarios, which are useful for workplace reflections.

Link: Mixed Conditionals

Why It’s Useful: The exercises encourage you to think about real-life situations, like “If I had prepared better, I wouldn’t be nervous now,” which mirrors workplace challenges.

💡 Tip: Complete the three online exercises to practice different types of mixed conditionals.

3. AgendaWeb

AgendaWeb offers a collection of mixed conditional exercises, including fill-in-the-blank and sentence-writing tasks. These are great for practicing the structure and applying it to various contexts.

Link: Mixed Conditional Exercises

Why It’s Useful: The site provides multiple quizzes that test your ability to choose the correct verb forms, helping you build accuracy in professional writing or speaking.

💡 Tip: Try the “Mixed conditionals quiz” to challenge yourself with different sentence types.

4. Perfect English Grammar

Perfect English Grammar offers clear explanations and interactive exercises for third and mixed conditionals, including downloadable PDFs for offline practice. The examples are straightforward and applicable to workplace scenarios.

Link: Conditional Exercises

Why It’s Useful: The site includes exercises like “If we had trained the staff, we wouldn’t be facing issues now,” which are relevant for discussing workplace mistakes.

💡 Tip: Download the PDF quizzes for extra practice you can do anytime.

5. GrammarBank

GrammarBank provides a range of mixed conditional exercises, including fill-in-the-blank and multiple-choice tasks, with answers for self-checking. The exercises focus on practical applications, making them ideal for vocational training students.

Link: Mixed Conditional Exercise

Why It’s Useful: The site’s exercises, like “If I had attended the meeting, I would know the plan now,” help you practice conditionals in professional contexts.

💡 Tip: Use the answer key to check your work and understand any mistakes.

🧠 How to Use These Resources

To get the most out of these websites:

  • Start with the explanations to review the grammar rules.
  • Complete the interactive exercises and check your answers to build accuracy.
  • Try rewriting the example sentences using workplace scenarios, like missed deadlines or client issues, to make them relevant to your studies.
  • Use the vocabulary from Unit 4 (e.g., “deal with,” “sort out”) in your answers to connect the grammar to professional communication.

Combine these online exercises with the “Mistakes at Work” video activities to practice conditionals in real-world contexts. The more you apply these structures, the more natural they’ll become!

💡 Tip: Set a goal to complete one exercise from each site and note down any new workplace-related examples you find.

Writing – Communicating Problems and Solutions

Why This Matters

When workplace issues arise, such as defective goods or customer complaints, clear and professional writing is crucial. This section teaches you to describe problems, explain their causes, and request or offer solutions, using Unit 4 grammar and vocabulary to communicate effectively.

💡 Tip: Write clearly to maintain trust and professionalism.

🎯 What You’ll Practice

You’ll develop writing skills through a practice task where you write to a supplier to request support after a problem, preparing you for situations like addressing suppliers or responding to customers. You’ll focus on:

  • Describing workplace issues clearly and professionally.
  • Using a formal tone and clear email structure.
  • Expressing regret or solutions with conditionals.
  • Using vocabulary for causes, consequences, and actions.

These skills, aligned with CEFR text production strategies, will enhance your professional communication.

💡 Tip: Structure your writing to be concise and polite.

🚀 Getting Started

The writing section includes a lesson to learn key strategies and phrases, followed by a practice task where you’ll write an email to a supplier. Start with the writing lesson to prepare, then move to the practice task to apply your skills.

💡 Tip: Plan your email (problem, cause, solution) for clarity.

Writing Lesson – Professional Emails

📝 Why Writing Matters

Writing clear, polite, and professional emails is a key skill in the workplace, whether you’re addressing a supplier about a problem or responding to a customer’s complaint. This lesson prepares you to write effective emails that describe issues, request solutions, or offer support, using a formal and reassuring tone.

You’ll develop CEFR text production strategies, such as planning your message, organizing it logically, and expressing your purpose clearly, to ensure your emails are professional and effective.

💡 Tip: Always consider who you’re writing to (e.g., supplier, customer) to choose the right tone and level of formality.

🧠 Key Language Tools

To write effective emails, you’ll use a range of language tools from Unit 4, including conditionals, polite requests, and vocabulary for problem-solving. These tools help you explain issues, show empathy, and propose solutions.

Tool Purpose Example
Conditionals (1st, 2nd, 3rd, Mixed) Explain possibilities, past mistakes, or current impacts If we had checked the order, we would have avoided this issue. (3rd)
If you confirm today, we can ship tomorrow. (1st)
Polite Requests Ask for action respectfully Please let us know how you plan to resolve this.
Unit 4 Vocabulary Describe problems and solutions We’re working on it to sort out the delay.
Wish / If Only Express regret or desired outcomes We wish the delivery had arrived on time.

💡 Tip: Combine conditionals with Unit 4 phrases like “deal with” or “follow up” to sound natural and professional.

✍️ Writing Scaffolding

Use this structure to write clear and professional emails for the practice and gradable tasks:

  • Greeting: Use a formal opening, e.g., “Dear [Name/Team].”
  • Introduction: Briefly state the purpose, e.g., “I’m writing regarding a recent delivery issue.”
  • Describe the Problem: Explain the issue clearly, using conditionals or “wish/if only” to show what happened or what could have been different (e.g., “If the goods had been inspected, this error would have been avoided.”).
  • Explain Impact or Context: Mention the consequences, possibly using a conditional (e.g., “If the delivery had arrived on time, we wouldn’t be delayed now.”).
  • Request or Offer Solution: Politely ask for action or propose a solution, e.g., “Please let us know how you can address this” or “We’d like to offer a refund.”
  • Closing: End with a polite sign-off, e.g., “Thank you for your attention. Best regards, [Your Name].”

Example Email (B2C):
Dear Ms. Smith,
I’m writing to address your recent complaint about a delayed delivery. We’re sorry for the inconvenience; if the courier had followed the schedule, you would have received your package on time. We wish this hadn’t happened. To resolve this, we’d like to offer a replacement or a full refund. Please let us know your preference.
Best regards,
[Your Name]

💡 Tip: Keep your email concise, using short sentences and clear language to ensure professionalism.

🎯 Preparing for the Tasks

The writing tasks will involve:

  • Practice Task (B2B): Writing an email to a supplier about defective goods or a delay, requesting a solution.
  • Gradable Task (B2C): Responding to a customer complaint about a delivery issue, apologizing, and proposing a solution.

To prepare:

  • Review Unit 4 vocabulary (e.g., “sort out,” “we’re working on it”) to describe problems and solutions.
  • Practice using conditionals to explain issues or possibilities, but don’t rely on them exclusively.
  • Focus on a polite, formal tone, using phrases like “We apologize for the inconvenience” or “Please let us know.”

💡 Tip: Before writing, plan your email by noting the problem, impact, and solution to ensure a clear structure.

Writing Practice – Request for Support (B2B)

🛠 Task Overview

Context: You work in the purchasing department. You’ve received defective goods or experienced a delay. Write an email to the supplier explaining the problem and requesting a solution.

Focus:

  • Grammar: Use second and third conditionals to describe hypothetical situations or past mistakes.
  • Function: Describe the problem clearly and ask for support or compensation.
  • Tone: Keep the email formal and professional, as this is a business-to-business (B2B) communication.

Example:
If the order had been double-checked, the mistake would have been avoided. Please let us know how you plan to fix the issue.

📝 How to Complete the Task

Write a short email (100–150 words) to the supplier. Post the your email on the corresponding forum. Follow these steps:

  • Greeting: Use a formal opening, e.g., “Dear [Supplier Name/Team].”
  • State the Problem: Explain the issue (e.g., defective goods or delay), using a second or third conditional to reflect on what could have been different, e.g., “If the items had been inspected, we would have avoided this issue.”
  • Request a Solution: Politely ask for action, using phrases like “Please let us know” or “We would appreciate your prompt response.”
  • Closing: End formally, e.g., “Thank you for your attention. Best regards, [Your Name].”

💡 Tip: Use Unit 4 vocabulary like “deal with” or “sort out” to sound professional, and keep sentences clear and concise.

Speaking – Reflecting and Responding to Problems at Work

Why This Matters

Workplace issues like delivery delays or client complaints are common. How you discuss them—with clarity and professionalism—sets you apart. This section helps you describe problems, reflect on what went wrong, and suggest solutions using Unit 4 grammar and vocabulary.

💡 Tip: Speak clearly to build trust in workplace conversations.

🎯 What You’ll Practice

You’ll hone your speaking skills through a practice task where you give advice to a colleague about a workplace issue, preparing you for situations like supporting a coworker or discussing incidents with a manager. You’ll focus on:

  • Structuring ideas clearly.
  • Using conditionals to reflect on causes and consequences.
  • Expressing feedback or regret constructively.
  • Maintaining a calm, professional tone.

These skills, aligned with CEFR spoken production strategies, will boost your confidence in professional settings.

💡 Tip: Practice to sound confident and supportive.

🚀 Getting Started

The speaking section includes a lesson to learn key strategies and phrases, followed by a practice task where you’ll record advice for a coworker. Start with the speaking lesson to prepare, then tackle the practice task to apply your skills.

💡 Tip: Outline your response (problem, reflection, advice) for clarity.

Speaking Lesson – Talking About Problems and Solutions at Work

Why This Matters

In professional settings, discussing workplace issues—whether with colleagues, supervisors, or clients—is a critical skill. Being able to clearly describe what went wrong, reflect on alternative actions, and propose actionable solutions demonstrates competence and professionalism. This lesson equips you with the tools to speak confidently about workplace challenges, using the grammar and vocabulary from Unit 4 to structure your ideas effectively.

By practicing these skills, you’ll be better prepared for real-world scenarios, such as explaining a project delay in a team meeting or offering advice to a coworker, enhancing your ability to communicate with clarity and poise.

Tip: Focus on being clear and professional, even in high-pressure situations, to build trust and credibility.

What You’ll Learn to Do

This lesson will help you develop the following speaking skills:

  • Describe a real or imagined workplace problem with precision and clarity.
  • Use conditional sentences (e.g., second and third conditionals) to reflect on past mistakes or suggest improvements.
  • Offer polite and constructive advice to coworkers to foster collaboration.
  • Speak with confidence and a clear structure, even when discussing challenging or stressful topics.

These skills are essential for professional communication, enabling you to contribute effectively in meetings, discussions, or client interactions.

Strategies for Effective Speaking

To help you speak clearly and professionally, this lesson incorporates CEFR-informed strategies for spoken production, such as planning, organizing ideas, and using connectors for coherence. Below are practical strategies to guide your preparation and performance:

Plan Before You Speak

Organize your thoughts using a simple outline to ensure your message is clear:

  • What happened? (Describe the problem, e.g., a delayed shipment.)
  • Why did it happen? (Explain the cause, e.g., a miscommunication.)
  • What was the result? (State the impact, e.g., a missed deadline.)
  • What could have been done differently? (Reflect using conditionals.)
  • What can we do next time? (Propose solutions or advice.)

Planning helps you stay focused and communicate your ideas logically.

Use Helpful Connectors

Connectors make your speech flow naturally and help your listener follow your ideas. Use these expressions:

Purpose Expressions
Describe the problem One issue we had was… / The problem started when…
Explain the cause It happened because… / The main cause was…
Describe the result As a result… / This affected… / It delayed…
Reflect on the past If we had… we would have… / It would have helped if…
Give advice Next time, you could… / If something similar happens, I suggest… / You should have…

Speak Clearly and Steadily

To communicate effectively:

  • Prioritize clarity over perfection—focus on getting your message across.
  • Pause briefly between ideas to give your listener time to process.
  • Emphasize key points by stressing important words (e.g., “The delivery was late, so we missed the deadline.”).

Practicing these techniques will help you sound confident and professional.

Tip: Practice these strategies with a partner or by recording yourself to refine your delivery.

Useful Phrases for Workplace Discussions

These phrases, drawn from Unit 4, will help you describe problems, reflect on actions, and propose solutions in a professional manner:

Category Phrases
Describing what went wrong There was a problem with the delivery / system / communication.
The incident occurred during the morning shift.
It wasn’t reported in time / It was overlooked.
Expressing regret or reflection If we had followed the procedure, this wouldn’t have happened.
It would have helped if someone had checked the labels.
We should have confirmed the details beforehand.
Giving advice to a coworker If I were you, I would…
You could try to… next time.
Next time, don’t hesitate to ask for help.
It’s a good idea to double-check before confirming anything.
Proposing solutions Next time, we can… / We should… / I recommend…
To avoid this in the future, we need to…
Let’s make sure we… before we…

Tip: Memorize a few phrases from each category to use them naturally during discussions.

Practice Tips for Success

To prepare for the speaking tasks, follow these steps to build confidence and fluency:

  • Practice out loud with a partner or alone to get comfortable with the phrases and structure.
  • Use scenario cards or real-life examples (e.g., a missed deadline, a technical error) to make practice relevant.
  • Record yourself speaking and listen back to evaluate clarity, structure, and tone. Ask: Is my message clear? Do I sound professional?
  • Prepare brief notes to guide your speaking, but avoid reading directly to maintain a natural delivery.
  • Incorporate Unit 4 vocabulary (e.g., “deal with,” “sort out,” “follow up”) to enhance fluency.

These techniques will help you refine your speaking skills and prepare for both practice and evaluated tasks.

Tip: Time yourself to ensure you can explain a problem and solution in 1–2 minutes, a common expectation in workplace discussions.

Preparing for the Practice Task

The speaking practice task will involve discussing a workplace problem, reflecting on what went wrong, and proposing solutions. To prepare:

  • Choose a scenario (e.g., a delayed delivery, a miscommunication with a client).
  • Follow the planning outline: describe the problem, cause, result, reflection, and solution.
  • Use conditionals to reflect (e.g., “If we had checked the schedule, we would have avoided the delay.”) and advice phrases (e.g., “Next time, I suggest confirming the details.”).
  • Practice delivering your response in a clear, professional tone, using connectors to organize your ideas.

This practice will build your confidence for real-world workplace conversations and prepare you for any evaluated speaking tasks.

Tip: Practice with a peer on the platform’s discussion forum to get feedback and improve.

Key Takeaways

This lesson is about more than grammar—it’s about mastering a critical communication skill. By learning to describe problems, reflect on past actions, and propose solutions clearly and professionally, you’ll be better equipped to handle workplace challenges and contribute to your team’s success.

Proceed to the practice section, select a scenario, and start speaking. Your ability to communicate effectively will set you apart in any professional environment.

Speaking Practice – Giving Advice to a Coworker

Task Overview

Context: A coworker has described a problem they faced during their shift and is asking for your advice on what they could have done differently and how to handle similar situations in the future.

Task: Individually, select a workplace scenario provided on the platform (e.g., via a scenario card or text description). Record a spoken response as if giving advice directly to your coworker. In your response:

  • Acknowledge the problem by summarizing it briefly to show understanding.
  • Explain how the situation could have been handled differently, using past-focused structures like third conditionals or “should have.”
  • Offer advice or solutions for the future, using first or second conditionals or phrases like “you could.”
  • Use a polite, supportive, and professional tone to maintain a collaborative approach.

This task develops CEFR-informed spoken production skills, such as organizing ideas clearly and delivering advice constructively, preparing you for professional workplace communication.

Tip: Imagine you’re speaking to a colleague in a supportive team discussion to keep your tone professional and encouraging.

Language Focus

Your response should incorporate these language elements from Unit 4:

  • Third Conditional: Reflect on alternative past actions (e.g., “If you had checked the inventory, the error might have been avoided.”).
  • First and Second Conditionals: Provide advice for future situations (e.g., “If this happens again, you could…” or “If I were you, I would…”).
  • Workplace Vocabulary: Use problem-solving terms like “deal with,” “sort out,” “escalate,” or “follow up” to sound professional and natural.

These language tools help you analyze past issues, propose actionable advice, and demonstrate empathy in a workplace context.

Tip: Use conditionals to add depth, but prioritize clear, simple advice to ensure your message is easy to follow.

How to Complete the Task

Prepare and record a short spoken response (1–2 minutes) for submission on the platform. Follow these steps:

  • Step 1 – Select a Scenario: Choose a workplace scenario from the platform’s resources (e.g., a delayed shipment, a client complaint, a technical issue). Read the description carefully, noting the problem, cause, and impact.
  • Step 2 – Plan Your Response: Organize your thoughts using this structure:
    • Acknowledge the problem (e.g., “I understand the client was frustrated due to the wrong order.”).
    • Reflect on the past (e.g., “If you had confirmed the details, the mistake could have been prevented.”).
    • Offer future advice (e.g., “Next time, you could double-check with the team to avoid errors.”).
  • Step 3 – Practice: Rehearse your response out loud to ensure clarity and a steady pace. Focus on a supportive tone and natural delivery.
  • Step 4 – Record and Submit: Record your response using the platform’s audio tool. Use brief notes to guide you, but speak naturally rather than reading. Submit the recording for feedback or self-review.

Example Response

I understand the issue with the delayed delivery caused a lot of stress during your shift. If you had contacted the logistics team earlier, the problem might have been resolved faster. In the future, I suggest escalating delays immediately to avoid client complaints. If something like this happens again, you could also keep the client updated to manage their expectations.

Tip: Listen to your recording to check for clarity, politeness, and structure before submitting.

Tips for Success

To deliver an effective response:

  • Begin with a supportive statement to show empathy (e.g., “That sounds like a challenging situation.”).
  • Use conditionals sparingly to avoid sounding critical; focus on constructive, forward-looking advice.
  • Incorporate Unit 4 vocabulary (e.g., “sort out,” “we’re working on it”) to enhance fluency and relevance.
  • Practice with multiple scenarios to build confidence in handling different workplace issues.
  • Ensure your recording is clear by speaking at a steady pace and pausing briefly between ideas.

These strategies will help you produce a professional, supportive response that mirrors real workplace communication.

Tip: Aim for a 1–2 minute response to keep it concise, as expected in professional discussions.

🎉 Unit 4 Complete: Learn from Every Challenge

🎉 Congratulations!

You’ve successfully completed Unit 4 – Problem solving at work!

🏆 Your Achievements

In this unit, you’ve developed key communication skills to manage problems, explain what went wrong, and offer professional solutions. You’ve:

  • Described common workplace problems and their consequences
  • Used conditional structures to explain, reflect and propose actions
  • Expanded your vocabulary to talk about support, incidents and communication breakdowns
  • Written a formal email in response to a customer complaint
  • Recorded a spoken message to report an incident and suggest improvements

🧠 Why Reflection Matters

Taking time to reflect helps you:

  • Recognise what you can already do — and what you want to improve
  • Strengthen your Competence to Learn to Learn
  • Support your Competence for Lifelong Learning
  • Build your confidence in complex and stressful workplace situations

Be honest. Celebrate your progress. Set your next communication goal.

📝 Personal Self-Assessment

🔤 Language & Communication

  • Can you describe a problem clearly and professionally?
  • Can you offer support or apologise using appropriate tone and expressions?
  • Do you feel more confident dealing with difficult situations in English?

📘 Grammar & Vocabulary

  • Can you use conditional structures to talk about real or imaginary scenarios?
  • Are you using vocabulary related to problems and solutions more fluently?
  • Can you explain causes and consequences in a structured way?

💡 Learning Process

  • Did the planning sheets help you organise your ideas before speaking or writing?
  • Did you apply feedback from previous tasks to improve your performance?
  • Have you started to reflect more actively on how you learn?

🚀 Looking Ahead

  • What communication skill helped you the most in this unit?
  • What do you want to keep improving in the next unit?
  • How can you continue building your confidence and flexibility at work?

👏 Keep Moving Forward

Every challenge is an opportunity. Well done on everything you’ve achieved. Take a moment to reflect, feel proud — and get ready for Unit 5. You’re growing into a stronger, more resourceful communicator every day.

Unit 4: Problem solving at work - Session 3
